General guides and procedures. Always refer to the procedures and standards required by your workplace and client.

About

This is a personal reference wiki with general survey guides and procedures. Always follow the specific procedures and standards required by your company and client. Content is manufacturer-neutral where possible, with specific equipment references where technically necessary.

docs.title: Welcome Aboard
docs.text:
This guide is an introduction to offshore surveying for people entering the field. It covers what the job involves, what the tools do, how teams work together, and what day-to-day life looks like on a survey vessel.

Offshore surveying sits at the intersection of technology, engineering, and the ocean. The work is varied, the learning curve is real, and no two projects are exactly alike. This guide gives you a foundation to build on.

docs.title: How to Use This Guide
docs.text:
Each chapter covers a core topic -- positioning, sensors, team roles, safety, data, and more. Some sections get technical, but the goal is always to orient you, not overwhelm you. When you want the full detail, follow the wiki links.

You can read straight through or jump to whatever's relevant. Need to brush up on positioning before tomorrow's survey? Go to that chapter. Want to understand what the ROV team does? Skip ahead.

How This Guide Connects to the Wiki

Throughout the chapters, you'll see "Go Deeper" boxes. These link to the wiki's detailed technical articles where you can read full procedures, calibration guides, and reference material. This guide is the starting point; the wiki has the detail.

docs.title: What's Inside
docs.text:
• Part I: Setting Sail

    What offshore surveying is, vessel life, team roles, safety, and regulations

    Start here

  • Part II: The Core Stuff

    Coordinate systems, positioning technologies, sensors, and sonars

    Get technical

  • Part III: Data

    Survey techniques, data processing, calibration, and verification

    Dive into data

  • Part IV: Modern Surveying

    Remote operations, IT basics, and AI tools for surveyors

    Go modern

  • Part V: Career & Mindset

    Soft skills, professional growth, mental health, and work-life balance

    Grow your career

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
AI has arrived in offshore surveying. Tools like ChatGPT can draft reports, write processing scripts, or suggest troubleshooting steps for common sensor issues. It's a digital assistant that speeds up repetitive tasks so you can focus on higher-level decisions and hands-on work.

As a trainee, experimenting with AI platforms will sharpen your skills. Just treat the output as a first draft, not gospel. When it comes to interpreting complex survey data or making project-altering recommendations, human judgment still calls the shots.

docs.title: A Note on Domain Knowledge
docs.text: Technology is just a tool to amplify what you already know. Honing your geodesy, subsea engineering, or acoustic positioning knowledge matters more than perfecting your programming skills. AI can handle some coding -- it can't replace your specialized insight into the marine environment.

docs.title: Writing Scripts and Code Snippets
docs.text:
One of the best uses for AI is getting it to help you create or refine scripts for automating routine tasks. Renaming a batch of side-scan sonar files by date, extracting specific time segments from a massive ROV video dataset -- these are small tasks that snowball fast when you're doing them a hundred times.

Script examples:

  • A file-renaming script in Python that reads your existing filenames and appends a date stamp
  • A data parser for survey logs that organizes depth readings into neat CSV files
  • A batch converter that takes raw sonar data and preps it for your favorite processing software

Your supervisor or data processor might point out the best candidates for automation. As a trainee, this is your chance to learn basic coding while spotting inefficiencies in the workflow. You're not deciding which systems get fully automated -- management and senior engineers own that call -- but your suggestions might spark real improvements.

docs.title: Brainstorming, Problem-Solving & Research Support
docs.text: Sometimes you just need a quick refresher on how a particular positioning system works or a concise explanation of industry jargon. AI assistants are good for summarizing concepts in plain language. That said, not everything AI produces is accurate. Use it as a starting point and verify crucial details through official manuals, onboard training materials, and experienced colleagues.

docs.title: Tips on Prompting an AI
docs.text:
• Be Specific: Instead of asking "How do I calibrate USBL?" try "Explain the standard box-in calibration process for Kongsberg HiPAP USBL systems in offshore surveys."
  • Provide Context: The more background you give the AI, the more relevant the response.
  • Double-Check Facts: If the result conflicts with your project manual, trust the manual. Or ask a senior team member to clarify.

docs.title: Best Practices: Verification, Ethics, and Creativity
docs.text:
AI is powerful but not infallible. Always verify critical information, especially when a wrong answer could derail a survey line or lead to faulty data interpretation. Ask yourself: how serious is it if the AI is wrong? The higher the stakes, the more human oversight is needed.

  1. Verification is Key: Review important outputs with a teammate or against your project documentation. If you're writing a script that could overwrite files, test it on a small subset first.
  2. Mind the Ethics: Don't feed confidential project info into public AI systems. Stick to sanitized or generic descriptions of the problem.
  3. Stay Creative: AI can jumpstart ideas, but humans excel at creativity and nuanced understanding. Use it to spark new scripts or report templates, then refine them with your professional insight.
  4. Know When to Ask for Help: If your script keeps failing or the AI's suggestions feel off, talk to your senior surveyor or Party Chief. A two-minute chat can save hours of head-scratching.

AI tools let you work faster and smarter, but they don't replace your knowledge of offshore surveying. Combine AI-driven efficiency with your growing domain expertise, and you become someone who not only uses the technology but actually understands what the data means and how to act on it.

Next up: Soft Skills & Professional Growth - because a successful offshore surveyor isn't just good with instruments and data.

docs.title: Why Calibration Matters
docs.text: Calibration is the process of fine-tuning your equipment so the data it produces is accurate and trustworthy. If your instruments are even slightly off, your data is off -- and inaccurate data leads to costly mistakes. Simple as that.

docs.title: Documents, Guidelines, and Procedure Manuals
docs.text:
Most companies follow internal or industry-recognized guidelines for calibration. In practice, you'll work with:

  • Project Procedure Documents: Custom documents prepared for a specific job, containing step-by-step instructions and acceptance criteria. If a colleague hands you a calibration checklist, keep it close.
  • Manufacturer Manuals: GNSS receivers, USBL systems, and MBES units all come with recommended calibration intervals and methods. The manufacturer's guidelines ensure you're aligning the system as designed.
  • Industry Standards: Organizations like IMCA provide general best practices for marine surveying. These tend to be high-level, but they're a solid baseline when your project documents aren't clear.

Read these documents thoroughly. They'll detail procedures like box-in tests for USBL or patch tests for MBES, and they specify tolerance levels for accuracy.

docs.title: Real-World Trainee Involvement
docs.text:
As a trainee, you might help record readings during a static GNSS test or set up reflectors for an MBES patch test. Senior surveyors or the Party Chief typically decide whether the system meets project specs or if more checks are needed, often after consulting with data processors and equipment specialists.

It Takes Time

It takes experience to spot when something is off. Early in your career, lean on procedure documents and ask "Does this value look right?" Over time, you'll develop an instinct for what normal ranges look like. A USBL check that's 2 meters off is a red flag. A few centimeters might be within tolerance.

Calibration is a great skill-builder. Every time you go through the process, you deepen your understanding of the equipment, the physics behind the measurements, and the standards that govern good data.

docs.title: Verification: Confirming Your Calibration Holds Up
docs.text:
Calibration is about getting things right. Verification is about confirming they stay right under real-world conditions. Both are part of maintaining data integrity.

You won't be the one deciding when or how these tests occur at first -- that's the project lead's call, based on client requirements and industry standards. But witnessing these tests teaches you the process and shows you why proper verification matters.

Go Deeper

The wiki has detailed step-by-step procedures for every calibration type:

USBL: HiPAP Calibration | Verification | Theory

INS/DVL: Calibration Guide | Sprint-Nav

MBES: Patch Test

Sound Velocity: SV Operations | SVP Repeatability

Attitude/Heading: AHRS Calibration | Gyro Types

docs.title: Documentation and Reporting
docs.text:
After calibrations and verification tests, results get documented. Reports detail system checks, observed biases, and corrective actions taken. Pay attention to how these reports are structured -- clear documentation makes life easier for everyone, including future surveyors who might revisit the data years later.

You might start by proofreading or adding basic info to a report. Over time, you'll learn to summarize findings and suggest follow-up actions. Major decisions (whether to re-calibrate or swap equipment) remain a team and management call, but your growing understanding will help you contribute meaningfully.

Go Deeper

  • Acceptance Criteria Reference
  • TPU and Error Budgets

docs.title: Troubleshooting: When Things Go Wrong
docs.text:
In offshore surveying, things go wrong. Count on it. A sensor starts spitting erratic readings, or your MBES data suddenly looks like noise. Troubleshooting is where experience, logic, and teamwork converge. You might be asked to check cables, reboot systems, or compare notes with a data processor to pinpoint the root cause.

As a trainee, you won't be deciding to abandon a survey line or switch to backup equipment just yet. But pay close attention during troubleshooting sessions. You'll see how problems get identified, communicated, and systematically worked through -- often under the pressure of ticking clocks and mounting costs.

These real-time problem-solving experiences build confidence. They also develop skills that aren't written in any manual -- skills you'll carry throughout your career.

Next up: Remote Surveying - the rise of remote operations and what it means for the future of offshore surveying.

docs.title: Understanding Earth's Shape
docs.text:
Earth isn't a perfect sphere. It's an oblate spheroid -- fatter at the equator, slightly flattened at the poles -- and its actual surface is irregular due to variations in gravity and mass distribution. The technical term for this gravity-defined shape is the geoid.

For practical surveying, we don't work with the geoid directly. Instead, we use mathematical models called ellipsoids that approximate Earth's shape, smoothing out the irregularities into something we can compute with. You don't need to memorize the math behind them, but you do need to know which ellipsoid and datum your project uses. Get that wrong and every position you record will be offset from reality.

docs.title: Datums, Projections, and Coordinate Systems
docs.text:
Mapping a 3D surface onto a flat screen or chart requires three concepts working together: datums, projections, and coordinate systems.

  • Datums define the reference framework for measurements. WGS84 is the most common global datum and the one used by GPS. Regional datums also exist (NAD83 in North America, ETRS89 in Europe) because a locally optimized model can be more accurate for a specific area.

  • Projections are methods for flattening the curved Earth onto a 2D surface. Every projection distorts something -- area, shape, distance, or direction. For offshore work, UTM (Universal Transverse Mercator) is the standard. UTM divides the world into 60 zones, each 6 degrees wide, giving you metric coordinates to work with.

  • Coordinate Systems tie it all together. Geographic coordinates (latitude and longitude in degrees) are used for global references. Projected coordinates (eastings and northings in meters) are what you'll use for day-to-day survey work.

Why This Matters

Getting the datum wrong means all your positions are off. Mixing up WGS84 and a local datum can introduce errors of tens or even hundreds of meters. Always confirm the coordinate reference system in the project specification before you start work.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Earth isn't a perfect sphere; we use ellipsoids and datums to approximate its shape for calculations
  • Always confirm which datum and projection your project uses before starting any work
  • WGS84 is the most common global datum you'll encounter; UTM is the most common projection for offshore work
  • Getting the datum wrong means all your positions are wrong, so double-check this early
  • When in doubt, ask your Party Chief or project lead which coordinate reference system the project spec requires

Go Deeper

For the full technical reference on coordinate systems, datums, and projections:

  • Coordinate Systems and Datums

Next up: Positioning Technologies -- from satellites in space to acoustic pulses underwater, how we figure out where things actually are.

docs.title: From Raw Data to Refined Results
docs.text:
You've collected gigabytes of data -- ping after ping of bathymetry, side scan sonar imagery, and sub-bottom profiles. Right now it's a messy collection of numbers and fuzzy images. The job of data processing is to turn all of that into polished charts, maps, and 3D models that people can actually use.

As a trainee, you'll start small: organizing files, running backups, confirming that data streams are recording properly. Over time you'll learn to use the software yourself -- filtering noise, applying corrections, aligning datasets. The senior staff and project leads set the processing standards and deliverable formats, but the hands-on skills you build here are the foundation for everything that comes later.

docs.title: Software Overview: Qinsy, Qimera, SIS, and More
docs.text:
Different tools serve different purposes in the processing chain:

  • Qinsy & Qimera: Widely used for acquisition and processing, turning raw bathymetry into crisp surfaces. Qinsy handles real-time data acquisition, while Qimera is its companion for post-processing and visualization.
  • SIS (Seafloor Information System): Often paired with Kongsberg multibeam systems, providing real-time visualization and data quality checks.
  • GIS (Geographic Information Systems): Software like ArcGIS or QGIS helps you layer and analyze spatial data, creating maps that combine survey results with other geographical information.

You won't know your way around these programs on day one. Start by watching how others use them. Pay attention to which menus they reach for, which parameters they adjust, and why they pick one tool over another for a given task. That awareness comes before proficiency -- and both come before mastery.

docs.title: Survey Manager in Qinsy
docs.text: One tool you'll encounter early is Qinsy's Survey Manager. This is the project setup module where you define your vessel, sensors, offsets, and coordinate systems -- everything the software needs before data acquisition begins. You're telling the system what equipment you have, where it sits on the vessel, and what coordinate reference the project uses. During mobilization, senior surveyors handle this setup. Watch closely, because understanding Survey Manager means understanding how all your equipment connects to the data.

docs.title: Creating Charts and 3D Visualizations
docs.text:
Processing isn't just about cleaning up noise. Your final deliverables go to clients who may not be surveyors. They need clear visuals: contour maps, shaded relief images, 3D perspectives of the seabed.

You'll learn to color-code depths, highlight features, and produce fly-through animations. The choice of visualization techniques usually involves input from clients, senior surveyors, and the project manager, but you can contribute by learning the tools well enough to suggest improvements and work efficiently.

A Note on Online vs Offline Work

Data processing and visualization are typically handled by the offline team or during post-survey phases. As a trainee, you'll see this more in the later stages of a project. The online team focuses on real-time acquisition and QC, while the offline team takes that raw data and refines it into deliverables. Both roles are essential, and understanding the handoff between them is key to smooth operations.

docs.title: CAD, GIS, and Beyond
docs.text:
Sometimes clients need more than maps. They need detailed plans, engineering drawings, or spatial analyses. That's where CAD (Computer-Aided Design) and GIS step in. CAD tools produce precise drawings of structures, routes, or layouts. GIS lets you analyze spatial relationships -- how close a pipeline sits to a protected reef, for example.

As a trainee, you'll likely start with basic GIS functions: layering data, adding labels, getting comfortable with the interface before tackling complex spatial queries. When decisions come up around coordinate systems for final deliverables or specialized analyses, project leads will consult with GIS specialists or engineers before locking anything in.

Go Deeper

  • Survey Reporting Fundamentals

Next up: Calibration & Verification - making sure your equipment is telling the truth.

docs.title: Computers, Networks, and Connectivity
docs.text:
You don't need to be an IT specialist, but a solid grasp of basic computer and network concepts will save you a lot of headaches. Understanding the difference between LAN (Local Area Network) and WAN (Wide Area Network), knowing how to troubleshoot a flaky Wi-Fi connection, or handling a minor software crash -- these skills keep you productive when problems come up.

As a trainee, you'll start with the basics:

  • Rebooting Hardware: Sometimes turning the system off and on really does fix the issue.
  • Checking Cable Connections: A loose Ethernet or USB cable can look like a serious failure -- until you plug it back in properly.
  • Verifying IP Addresses: If you can't connect to a device or server, confirm you're on the correct subnet and IP range.

Decisions about vessel network architecture or advanced cybersecurity fall to IT specialists and management. But your willingness to learn and handle smaller problems makes you a more valuable team member.

docs.title: Managing Software & Updates
docs.text:
Not glamorous, but staying on top of software updates is key to smooth operations. Survey applications evolve rapidly, introducing new features or fixing bugs that could affect data quality or workflow. Senior staff typically schedule major upgrades and test new versions before rolling them out. You can still help by:

  • Reporting Glitches Promptly: If you notice odd behavior -- a button that doesn't work, a sudden crash -- tell someone. Early reporting can prevent others from losing work.
  • Tracking Version Numbers: Keep a record of which versions of key software are installed. It's easy to forget whether the echo sounder software got updated last month or last year.
  • Flagging Minor Issues: Even small hiccups (like a misaligned GUI element) can be clues to bigger compatibility problems. A quick screenshot and note helps senior staff determine if it's a one-off or a sign of something deeper.

Over time, you'll learn which updates are routine and which need careful coordination (like major OS changes). Staying aware of this makes you a useful resource for version-related questions.

docs.title: Cybersecurity Basics
docs.text:
Phishing attacks, malware, ransomware -- these threats are real and can compromise sensitive project data fast. You're not expected to deploy firewalls or develop security policy, but you play a role by:

  • Avoiding Suspicious Emails: If an email looks off -- weird grammar, unexpected attachments, alarming links -- consult your supervisor or IT.
  • Never Sharing Passwords: It's tempting to give a colleague your login "just this once," but that undermines security protocols.
  • Reporting Anything Odd: A strange pop-up, a suspicious external drive -- speak up. Small anomalies can be early signs of larger security risks.

Your company's reputation depends on safeguarding data, and a single misstep can cost millions or damage client relationships. Even basic habits like logging out of your workstation when you step away keep the entire project safer.

docs.title: Troubleshooting Common Tech Issues
docs.text:
Technology hiccups are inevitable. A calm approach and a few basic troubleshooting steps make all the difference:

  1. Restart Software: Closing and reopening the application often clears temporary glitches.
  2. Check Cable Connections: Jiggling a loose connector or swapping a questionable cable can restore functionality in seconds.
  3. Verify Licenses and Resources: Some survey applications require valid licenses or might run slowly if your CPU or RAM is maxed out. A quick look at Task Manager (Windows) or Activity Monitor (macOS) can reveal if a program is hogging resources.
  4. Close Unneeded Tasks: If your PC starts lagging during a survey, check running processes. Shutting down non-essential apps frees up memory and CPU.
  5. Document What You Tried: Write down the steps you took, even if they didn't work. This helps the next person pick up where you left off without duplicating effort.

If these steps don't resolve the issue, or if the problem is more serious (network outage, corrupted data), call in the experts. Stay calm, follow standard procedures, and learn from the situation. Over time, you'll build a troubleshooting instinct that serves you whether you're onshore or at sea.

Next up: AI Tools for Surveyors - how artificial intelligence is changing the way we work offshore.

docs.title: Vessel Terminology
docs.text:
Offshore vessels have their own language. Knowing these terms keeps you safe and lets you follow instructions without hesitation.

  • Bow: The front of the vessel. Mnemonic: it leads the way.
  • Stern: The rear. If you can feel the engine vibrations, you're near the stern.
  • Port: The left side when facing forward. "Port" and "left" both have four letters.
  • Starboard: The right side when facing forward.
  • Aft: Toward the stern.
  • Bridge: The vessel's command center -- navigation and overall control happen here. You'll hear radio calls like "Bridge, Survey here..."
  • Deck: The floor. Calling it "the floor" marks you as new.
  • Galley: The kitchen and dining area, sometimes called the mess.
  • Cabin/Berth: Your sleeping quarters. Small, but home for the duration.
  • Bulkhead: A wall. "Check the bulkhead" means check the wall.
  • Head: The bathroom. On a ship, you ask for the head.
  • Mess Hall: The dining and seating area, sometimes used interchangeably with galley.
  • Muster Station: Your designated meeting point during drills or emergencies.

Common phrases:

  • "Secure It": Tie it down or lock it in place. Things move at sea.
  • "Heads Up": A warning or a way to get attention for an announcement.
  • "Copy That": "I heard and understood."

These aren't just jargon -- they're how the vessel communicates. When someone says "move that gear to the port side, then head to the galley," you need to know what that means without thinking about it.

docs.title: Online vs Offline
docs.text:
On a survey vessel, "online" and "offline" don't refer to internet connectivity. They describe two distinct roles in the survey workflow:

  • Online team: The surveyors managing live, real-time operations. If you're online, you're monitoring positioning, running data acquisition, managing equipment during active survey work, and communicating directly with the bridge and ROV team. You're keeping the live data flowing and verifying it as it comes in.

  • Offline team: Data processors, geophysicists, video reviewers, and other specialists who work on data after acquisition. They clean, process, QC, and prepare deliverables. The offline team may be working simultaneously on the vessel or remotely from shore.

As a new surveyor, you'll likely start online -- watching and assisting with live operations. It's the fastest way to learn how everything connects.

docs.title: Communication Protocols
docs.text: Offshore vessels are noisy environments. Engines, waves, equipment -- without clear communication protocols, critical information gets lost. Radio discipline exists for a reason.

docs.title: Keep It Clear and Simple
docs.text:
Use call-and-response format. For example:

  • You: "Bridge, Survey."
  • Bridge: "Bridge, go ahead."
  • You: "Request permission to deploy equipment at the stern."
  • Bridge: "Bridge, understood, you may deploy."
  • You: "Survey, copy."

It might feel formal at first, but this structure confirms who is speaking, who is being spoken to, and what's being asked. It ensures the message is received and acknowledged -- not lost in radio static or misheard over engine noise.

docs.title: Use the Right Terms
docs.text: Standard nautical and survey terminology removes ambiguity. Everyone knows "aft" means toward the stern and "port" means left. Using the correct terms prevents miscommunication that could lead to safety incidents or costly mistakes.

docs.title: Teamwork
docs.text: Every role on a survey vessel depends on the others. Surveyors, ROV pilots, data processors, and deck crew all need to work in sync. When they do, operations run smoothly. When they don't, things slow down or go wrong.

docs.title: Know Each Other's Roles
docs.text:
Understanding what other team members need makes you more useful. If you know the ROV pilot needs the launch and recovery system prepped before a dive, you can anticipate that. If the data processor expects files in a particular folder structure, set it up that way from the start. Small things like this save time and reduce friction.

  • See the ROV technician struggling with wiring? Grab some zip ties or pass them a tool. It takes a minute and it builds trust.
  • Know how the data processor likes their files organized? Set it up that way without being asked. It saves back-and-forth later.

Ask questions about what others do. Most people are happy to explain their work, and it gives you a much better understanding of the full operation.

docs.title: Offer Help, Ask for Help
docs.text: If someone's wrestling with heavy gear or stuck on a problem, help out. If you're stuck, ask. Nobody expects you to know everything when you're starting out. Trust forms when people know they can count on each other.

docs.title: Watch and Learn
docs.text: As a beginner, observe how senior team members handle tasks. Much of this job is about patterns -- the sequence for powering equipment up and down, how the ROV gets positioned to avoid tangling its tether, which checks happen before a dive. Pay attention and you'll pick it up faster than you expect.

docs.title: Keep It Professional
docs.text: Good morale matters on a vessel where you're living and working with the same people for weeks. A well-timed joke helps. Humor that puts someone down or distracts from safety does not.

docs.title: Respect the Chain of Command
docs.text:
The Party Chief or Offshore Manager sets priorities and makes the calls. That doesn't mean you can't suggest improvements -- but do it respectfully, in the right setting. Constructive feedback is welcome; public confrontations are not. Keep in mind the client may be nearby, so stay professional.

Teamwork on a vessel isn't optional. It's the backbone of every successful offshore operation.

docs.title: The Offshore Routine
docs.text: Offshore life has its own rhythm. Work, rest, meals -- all structured around the operation and the shift pattern. Here's what helps:

docs.title: Stick to the Schedule
docs.text: You'll likely work 12-hour shifts. Protect your sleep. If you're on days, don't stay up late in the mess hall. If you're on nights, get blackout curtains or an eye mask. Consistent sleep makes everything else easier.

docs.title: Eat Well and Hydrate
docs.text: Offshore galleys serve big meals. Don't overdo it on fried food -- feeling sluggish doesn't help when you need to stay sharp. Drink water. The air conditioning dries you out, and dehydration causes headaches.

docs.title: Use Your Downtime
docs.text:
Read, watch something, socialize, or just have some quiet time. Find what works for you. Downtime is part of the job -- use it to recharge, not just kill time.

The people who settle into offshore life quickest are the ones who find their routine and stick to it.

Go Deeper

When it's time to prepare for your trip offshore, check the wiki's detailed checklists:

  • Mobilisation Checklist
  • Demobilisation Checklist

Next up: Staying Safe -- PPE, emergency procedures, environmental responsibility, and key regulations.

docs.title: Dealing with Stress & Anxiety
docs.text:
The offshore environment can be intense. Long hours, isolation from family, and the pressure to meet deadlines -- these things add up. It's worth acknowledging that rather than pretending everything's fine.

Coping strategies are personal, but some basics help: getting out on deck during a break, keeping in touch with people back home, having a routine outside of work hours. Small things, but they make a difference when you're weeks into a rotation.

If it gets overwhelming, reach out. Talk to a supervisor, the medical officer onboard, or use company-provided support lines. Recognizing when you need help is not a weakness -- it's just practical. Ignoring problems doesn't make them go away, and the people around you have likely dealt with similar things.

docs.title: Fitness & Nutrition
docs.text:
Vessel galleys serve plenty of food -- sometimes too much of the wrong kind. Maintaining a reasonable diet helps keep your energy steady through long shifts. You don't need to be extreme about it, just pay attention to what you're eating.

Exercise is similar. Even simple onboard routines -- push-ups, resistance bands, yoga, whatever works for you -- make a noticeable difference to your mood, mental clarity, and how well you sleep. Most vessels have a gym of some sort. Use it.

docs.title: Remote Work Boundaries
docs.text:
If you're in a shore-based or remote role, the line between "on duty" and "off duty" can blur fast. It's easy to check one more dataset or answer one more email when your workstation is in your living room. Set clear boundaries. Decide when your workday ends and stick to it. Communicate your availability to the team.

This isn't about being lazy -- it's about sustainability. Burning out doesn't help anyone, least of all you.

docs.title: Finding Fulfillment
docs.text:
Offshore surveying takes you to places most people never see. You'll work with technology that pushes the boundaries of what we know about the underwater world. Some mornings the sunrise over open ocean genuinely stops you in your tracks.

That said, not every day feels meaningful. Some days are just long shifts and repetitive tasks. Finding satisfaction in your work means taking the good with the ordinary -- celebrating the wins, learning from mistakes, and recognizing that every project is building your capability as a professional. The tough days are part of it. They shape you as much as the good ones.

You've reached the end of the guide. Check out Resources & Further Reading for reference tables, industry links, and recommended training. And don't forget: the rest of this wiki is packed with detailed procedures and technical guides for when you're ready to go deeper.

docs.title: GNSS, RTK, and INS
docs.text:
On the surface, positioning relies on satellites. Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) -- GPS (U.S.), GLONASS (Russia), Galileo (Europe), BeiDou (China) -- provide your primary position fix. Your receiver calculates position by measuring signal travel times from multiple satellites overhead.

Standard GNSS gets you within a few meters. When you need centimeter-level accuracy, RTK (Real-Time Kinematic) closes the gap. RTK uses a base station on a known point to broadcast correction data to your receiver, bringing positional accuracy down to a few centimeters.

No system is perfect, though. Signals can bounce off waves, degrade in bad weather, or drop out entirely. INS (Inertial Navigation System) uses accelerometers and gyroscopes to track movement independently. When GNSS signals drop out, the INS bridges the gap until satellites are reacquired. The longer it runs without external corrections, the more it drifts -- but for short outages, it keeps your position solution alive.

Go Deeper

  • GNSS Fundamentals
  • GNSS Jamming and Spoofing
  • DGNSS Integrity Check

docs.title: USBL and LBL: Underwater Positioning with Sound
docs.text: Radio waves don't propagate through water, so subsea positioning uses acoustics instead. The two most common methods offshore are USBL (Ultra-Short Baseline) and LBL (Long Baseline).

docs.title: USBL (Ultra-Short Baseline)
docs.text:
How it works: A transducer on the vessel sends an acoustic pulse, and a beacon on the ROV or subsea equipment replies. The system measures the angle and time delay of the return signal to calculate the beacon's position in 3D space.

Typical use: USBL is quick to deploy, making it the standard choice for routine inspections, shorter projects, or shallower water depths where real-time positioning is needed.

Why it's common offshore: Most vessels already have a USBL transducer pole installed, and the system integrates easily with standard survey software. It's your go-to when you need to start work fast or won't be staying in one location long.

docs.title: LBL (Long Baseline)
docs.text:
How it works: Multiple transponders are placed on the seabed, creating an underwater reference network. Each transponder responds when interrogated, and the system triangulates position by measuring ranges to each one.

When it's used: LBL is suited to permanent or high-precision operations -- deepwater drilling, long-term construction campaigns -- where you need extremely stable, repeatable positioning.

Why it's worth the effort: Setting up an LBL array takes more time (laying out and calibrating transponders), but the payoff is centimeter-level accuracy in environments where USBL alone can't deliver the required precision.

Both systems use the same underlying principle: measuring acoustic travel times through water to determine position.

Go Deeper

  • USBL Theory and Error Budgets
  • HiPAP USBL Calibration
  • HiPAP Verification (Spin & Transit)

Go Deeper

  • LBL Acoustic Positioning Fundamentals

docs.title: DVL, MRU, and Sound Velocity
docs.text: Positioning isn't just about where you are. It's also about how you're moving and how the water column affects your acoustic signals.

docs.title: DVL (Doppler Velocity Log)
docs.text:
A DVL sends acoustic beams toward the seabed and measures the Doppler shift of the return signals. This gives you speed and direction of movement relative to the ocean floor.

DVLs can be mounted on both vessels and ROVs (especially useful when operating near the seabed). They provide real-time velocity data that feeds into INS and other positioning systems to improve overall accuracy.

Go Deeper

  • INS Theory and Principles
  • INS/DVL Calibration Guide

docs.title: MRU (Motion Reference Unit)
docs.text:
The MRU tracks pitch, roll, and heave -- all the motion your vessel or ROV experiences. Without motion compensation, sensor data from systems like multibeam echo sounders would be distorted by the platform's movement.

  • On a vessel: MRUs provide the motion corrections needed to produce accurate bathymetry and positioning data.
  • On an ROV: Motion sensors on the vehicle itself feed orientation data into the positioning solution for accurate subsea work.

Go Deeper

  • Heave, MRU Theory and Verification

docs.title: Sound Velocity
docs.text:
Sound doesn't travel at a fixed speed underwater. Temperature, salinity, and depth all affect propagation speed, and these properties change with depth through the water column.

Where it matters:

  • Acoustic Positioning (USBL/LBL): Travel time calculations depend directly on knowing the speed of sound through the water column.
  • Sonar Systems (Multibeam, Side-Scan): Incorrect sound velocity profiles warp beam angles and distort the final data.

SVPs (Sound Velocity Profiles) and SV Sensors: You measure the speed of sound at various depths by lowering an SV probe through the water column. Some vessels have continuous sensors installed, but most operations also run periodic SVP casts to capture current conditions.

Skipping sound velocity corrections leads to warped or misaligned survey data. Regular updates keep your acoustic measurements accurate -- and accuracy is the whole point of the job.

docs.title: Integrating Systems
docs.text:
No single positioning system covers every requirement. In practice, you combine them. GNSS provides the surface position. INS maintains continuity through signal outages. USBL or LBL positions subsea targets. DVL measures velocity near the seabed. MRU compensates for platform motion. Sound velocity corrections keep the acoustics honest.

When these systems are properly integrated and calibrated, each one fills in the gaps the others can't cover. The result is a reliable, continuous positioning solution from the surface to the seabed -- which is what every survey operation depends on.

Next up: Sensors and Sonars -- your underwater toolkit, from multibeam echo sounders to ROVs and AUVs.

docs.title: The Rise of the Remote Surveyor
docs.text: Running an offshore survey from an onshore office -- or even your living room -- is no longer a novelty. It's becoming part of mainstream operations. Reliable high-speed data links, secure cloud storage, and advanced remote-access software now allow survey teams to monitor and control aspects of a job without being physically aboard the vessel.

docs.title: What We Gain
docs.text:
• Cost & Efficiency: Having part of the team onshore lowers travel expenses and reduces downtime. If you can troubleshoot equipment or process data in near-real time from a land-based office, you cut out the wait for the next crew change or the need to send additional specialists offshore.
  • 24/7 Collaboration: Remote staff often work in different time zones, keeping data processing going around the clock. When the offshore crew finishes a shift, the onshore team picks up where they left off.
  • Safer Conditions: Fewer people physically on the vessel means reduced exposure to harsh weather and potential safety hazards.
  • Environmental Benefits: Fewer crew flights and boat transfers means a lower carbon footprint. Remote operations reduce the need for helicopter or vessel-based personnel transfers, aligning with the industry's growing focus on sustainability.

docs.title: What We Lose
docs.text:
• Tangible Vessel Experience: Part of the traditional offshore role is feeling the motion of the vessel and experiencing the environment firsthand. Onshore, you can't judge sea conditions by stepping onto the deck, and you lose the spontaneous face-to-face conversations that often lead to creative problem-solving.
  • Connectivity Dependency: If the satellite link goes down, so does your ability to monitor or assist. Redundancy matters, but not all vessels have backup systems in place.

docs.title: Still a Team Sport
docs.text:
Remote surveying doesn't replace the offshore crew -- it complements them. Critical decisions (like adjusting the survey plan due to unexpected weather) still come from the vessel's leadership, often in consultation with onshore leads. Remote staff handle tasks like data processing, troubleshooting sensor issues, and offering analytical support in near real-time. This division of labor saves costs and frees offshore personnel to focus on immediate operational needs.

As a trainee, working from shore might feel counterintuitive. But it's just another way of collaborating. The vessel crew and the onshore office are part of the same team, spread across different spaces and time zones. And offshore jobs aren't vanishing anytime soon -- remote surveying is adding flexibility to how the work gets done, not replacing the vessel-based role.

docs.title: Virtual Machines & Network Drives: Setting Up Your Remote Office
docs.text: A Remote Operations Center (ROC) is where it comes together onshore: large monitors displaying live vessel positions, sensor readouts, and real-time communications, all without being on the ship. Virtual machines (VMs) and network drives make this possible. You can run specialized survey applications on powerful remote servers while cloud storage keeps data backed up and accessible globally.

docs.title: What a Remote Operations Center Might Look Like
docs.text:
• Centralized Monitoring: Large screens (or a video wall) showing vessel status, ROV camera feeds, and sensor dashboards in real time.
  • Team Collaboration: Operators, data processors, and supervisors seated side-by-side or connected through secure networks, sharing files and communicating instantly.
  • High-Speed Connectivity: Dedicated fiber lines or satellite uplinks ensuring low-latency access to vessel systems.

docs.title: Your Role as a Trainee
docs.text:
• Learning Remote Platforms: You might begin by learning how to log into a VM to access survey software like Qinsy or SIS. This involves understanding VPNs, secure protocols, and user credentials.
  • Managing Data: You'll likely help organize or retrieve datasets from network drives, making sure files are labeled correctly and stored in the right project folders.
  • Following Guidelines: Senior IT staff decide which cloud provider to use or how robust a VM setup needs to be. Your job is to follow security protocols and ask questions if something seems off.
  • Observing Real-Time Ops: If your company has a dedicated ROC, you might sit in during a live operation. Watching the onshore team coordinate with offshore personnel teaches you a lot about troubleshooting and maintaining smooth communication.

docs.title: Secure File Transfers and Cloud Services
docs.text: Offshore teams frequently use VMs hosted in the cloud to run demanding survey software. This lets you log in from a remote location while leveraging powerful server hardware. Large data files (bathymetric grids, ROV video) may be stored in cloud drives for redundancy and collaboration. When the vessel has a decent internet connection, you can upload or download the latest survey data without clogging local storage.

docs.title: Bandwidth and Satellite Links
docs.text:
Historically, transferring large files offshore meant dealing with painfully slow satellite links. Newer solutions like Starlink bring high-speed, low-latency internet to remote vessels. While not yet universal, Starlink and similar technologies drastically reduce transfer times, enabling near real-time processing and troubleshooting from onshore. That said, bandwidth can still fluctuate depending on location and satellite coverage, so being mindful of data sizes and transfer schedules remains important.

As you progress, you'll learn to manage file transfers effectively and securely. You might coordinate with IT staff to schedule large uploads during off-peak hours, or become the person responsible for organizing folder structures and naming conventions on the cloud server. Handling data responsibly shows attention to detail -- something managers notice when considering who's ready for more responsibility.

docs.title: Communication Tools & Virtual Collaboration
docs.text:
Modern collaboration tools (video conferencing, instant messaging, shared dashboards) let distributed teams work together seamlessly. The key difference to understand is where different roles typically sit:

  • Online surveyors managing real-time operations are more likely to be in a ROC or physically offshore. This setup ensures they're connected to the vessel's live data streams and can communicate immediately with the Party Chief or ROV team.
  • Offline processors working on data after acquisition have more flexibility and may work from a dedicated office or, in some cases, from home for tasks like cleaning point clouds or generating preliminary maps.

Regardless of where you're sitting, clear and proactive communication remains crucial. If something feels off, ask questions. If you need help, say so. The best way to avoid misunderstandings -- onshore or offshore -- is to speak up early.

Next up: IT Basics - computers, networks, and cybersecurity for the offshore professional.

docs.text:
